title: Mocking Basics
intro: Your function calls an external API. You can't (and shouldn't) call the real API in your tests -- it's slow, costs money, and might be down. Imagine using a stunt double in a movie instead of the real actor for dangerous scenes -- that's what mocking does for your code. A test suite is hitting a real API and causing problems. Figure out the solution.

teach.title: Mocking = Using a Stunt Double
teach.body: In a movie, dangerous scenes use stunt doubles instead of the real actor. The stunt double looks the same, acts the same role, but you control exactly what happens. **Mocking** is using a stunt double for your code's dependencies. Instead of calling a real API, you create a fake version that returns exactly what you want.

teach.title: Mocking = A Flight Simulator
teach.body: Pilots don't learn to fly by jumping into a real plane during a thunderstorm. They use flight simulators that create realistic scenarios without real danger. **Mocking** is a flight simulator for your code. You simulate the database, the API, the payment system -- testing every scenario (including crashes and failures) without touching anything real.

teach.title: Why Mock?
teach.body:
You mock dependencies because:

1. **Speed** -- Real API calls take 100ms+. Mocks take 0ms.
2. **Reliability** -- Real APIs can be down or rate-limited. Mocks always work.
3. **Control** -- You can simulate errors, empty responses, and edge cases.
4. **Cost** -- Some APIs charge per request. Don't waste money on tests.
5. **Isolation** -- You're testing YOUR code, not the API.

teach.title: Before and After Mocking
teach.annotation: The mock returns fake data instantly. Your test runs in milliseconds and works even when the API is down.

teach.body: Your vibecoded app probably talks to APIs, databases, or payment providers. Without mocking, you can't test these integrations safely. With mocking, you can verify every scenario -- including payment failures -- before a real customer's money is on the line.
Mocking replaces real dependencies (APIs, databases) with fake versions you control. This makes tests fast, reliable, and able to simulate any scenario including errors.

start.narrative: Your team's test suite was running fine until today. Now 5 tests are failing with 'Error: Request timeout -- api.weather.com did not respond'. The tests call a real weather API. The API appears to be having an outage. Your CI/CD pipeline is blocked -- no deploys until tests pass. What do you do?

choices.text: Mock the weather API in tests so they don't depend on the real service
Skip the failing tests until the API comes back online
Wait for the API to come back and re-run the tests

mock-api.narrative: Good thinking! You replace the real API calls with mocks. Tests pass again. A teammate asks: 'If we mock the API, how do we know our code actually works with the real API?'

choices.text: Unit tests use mocks; a few integration tests hit the real API on a separate schedule
We don't -- mocks are always sufficient

skip-tests.narrative: You skip the failing tests and deploy. An hour later, a code change breaks the weather display -- the function crashes on the API response format. The skipped tests would have caught it (mocks return the expected format).

choices.text: Roll back, set up mocks instead, and re-enable the tests

wait.narrative: Three hours later, the API is still down. The pipeline is frozen. An urgent bugfix is stuck waiting on a third-party outage you don't control. This will keep happening as long as tests reach across the network.

choices.text: Stop waiting -- mock the API so the suite is hermetic

design-mocks.narrative: You decide to mock. But how do you shape the mock so the tests actually catch real bugs? It's tempting to just hardcode { temp: 72 } and move on.

choices.text: Define separate mocks for success, error (500), timeout, and empty payload
One success mock with a realistic shape -- errors are rare
Return whatever shape makes the current tests pass

verify-fidelity.narrative: Now the danger: your mock could drift from reality. The real API might add a field, rename one, or change a status code, and your green tests would lie. How do you keep mocks honest?

choices.text: Run a small nightly contract test against the real API and alert on diffs
Eyeball the API docs every few months and update mocks if needed
Trust the mock -- the API never changes much

use-in-tests.narrative: You have realistic, contract-checked mocks. Now write the actual unit test. The component fetches weather and renders the temperature. What does a good test assert?

choices.text: Mock the API, render the component, assert the temperature appears in the DOM
Mock the API, call the function, assert the mock was called once
Snapshot the rendered output and trust future diffs to flag problems

end-good.narrative: Your suite has hermetic mocks for success, errors, timeouts, and empty payloads, plus a nightly contract test that pings the real API and warns if the shape changes. The component test asserts user-visible behavior: 'when the API returns 72, the page shows 72'. The pipeline is unblocked, fast, and trustworthy.
end-good.feedback: Excellent. Mocks made tests fast, multiple scenarios made them honest, contract testing kept them aligned with reality, and behavior assertions made them meaningful. This is exactly how mature teams handle external dependencies.

end-okay.narrative: Tests pass and the pipeline is unblocked. But your assertion checks that the mock was called -- not that the user sees the right thing. If a refactor breaks the rendering but still hits the API, the test stays green and the bug ships.
end-okay.feedback: Mocking strategy was solid. The weak spot is asserting on implementation details (the mock was called) instead of user-visible outcomes. Always test what the user experiences, not what the code happens to do internally.

end-poor.narrative: Snapshots make the test green, but every cosmetic change triggers a snapshot diff and nobody knows what's intentional. Worse, your snapshot was taken with a stale mock shape -- the test will keep passing even if the real API changes underneath you.
end-poor.feedback: Snapshots without behavior assertions document the past, not the contract. Combined with thin mocks, you get tests that pass while production breaks. Assert on behavior, mock multiple scenarios, and check mock fidelity against reality.
